chapter_title: Appendix A: IAP Standard Quick Reference Card
section_title: 1. IAP ACVIP 2024-2025: At-a-Glance Primary Immunization Schedule
content_md:
## 1. IAP ACVIP 2024-2025: At-a-Glance Primary Immunization Schedule

*This represents the recommended private schedule from the Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP). The Government of India Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) is available at government clinics as a free alternative.*

| Age | Vaccines per IAP | Key Notes / Guidance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Birth** | BCG, OPV-0, Hepatitis B-1 | Give within 24 hours of birth; plus Vitamin K1 (1 mg IM in right thigh) |
| **6 Weeks** | DTwP/DTaP-1, IPV-1, Hib-1, Hep B-2, Rotavirus-1, PCV-1 | PCV is PCV13/15; Rotavirus has a strict 32-week upper age limit |
| **10 Weeks** | DTwP/DTaP-2, IPV-2, Hib-2, Hep B-3, Rotavirus-2, PCV-2 | DTwP (whole-cell) is preferred; DTaP (acellular) has fewer reactions |
| **14 Weeks** | DTwP/DTaP-3, IPV-3, Hib-3, Hep B-4, Rotavirus-3, PCV-3 | Completes the primary polio and rotavirus series |
| **6 Months** | Influenza-1 | Trivalent formulation from 2025 (B/Yamagata lineage retired) |
| **7 Months** | Influenza-2 | Give 4 weeks after first dose; then annual influenza vaccine until age 5 |
| **6–9 Months** | Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV) | Single dose of TCV; no booster required under current standards |
| **9 Months** | MMR-1 | Replaces MR (Measles-Rubella) to include Mumps protection |
| **12 Months** | Hepatitis A-1 | Inactivated (2-dose) or live (single-dose) vaccine |
| **15 Months** | MMR-2, Varicella-1, PCV-Booster | MMR-2 completes the mumps coverage; PCV-booster covers pneumococcal |
| **16–18 Months**| DTwP/DTaP-B1, Hib-B1, IPV-B1 | First booster series for diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, Hib, and polio |
| **18–19 Months**| Hepatitis A-2, Varicella-2 | Hep A-2 is required for inactivated series; Varicella-2 covers chickenpox |
| **4–6 Years** | DTwP/DTaP-B2, IPV-B2, MMR-3 | Pre-school booster series |
| **9–15 Years** | HPV (Human Papillomavirus) | **Girls**: Single dose (immunocompetent); **Boys**: 2 doses at 0 and 6 months |
| **10 Years** | Tdap | Booster for tetanus, diphtheria, and acellular pertussis |
| **15–18 Years** | HPV (if missed earlier) | 3-dose series at 0, 2, and 6 months |
| **16–18 Years** | Td or Tdap | Adolescent tetanus and diphtheria booster |
| **Pregnancy** | Tdap | Single dose of Tdap at 27–36 weeks of **every** pregnancy |

---

7 Months Influenza-2 Give 4 weeks after first dose; then annual influenza vaccine until age 5

12 Months Hepatitis A-1 Inactivated (2-dose) or live (single-dose) vaccine

chapter_title: Appendix A: IAP Standard Quick Reference Card
content_md:
## 2. IAP & Pediatric Primary Care Standards

*A summary of key clinical guidelines for non-vaccine medical and safety issues.*

| Topic | IAP & Pediatric Standard | Safety Callouts & Practical Advice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Breastfeeding** | Exclusive for 6 months; continue for 2 years or beyond. | Aligns with WHO/UNICEF BFHI 2018. Initiate skin-to-skin during the "Golden Hour." |
| **Vitamin D** | 400 IU/day oral Vitamin D3 starting from birth. | Mandatory for all breastfed and partially breastfed infants. |
| **Iron** | 1 mg/kg/day starting at 4 months for term breastfed babies. | 2 mg/kg/day starting at 1 month for preterm/low-birth-weight babies. |
| **Vitamin K** | 1 mg Vitamin K1 by intramuscular injection at birth. | Universal birth dose to prevent Vitamin K Deficiency Bleeding (VKDB). |
| **Vitamin A** | 9 doses from 9 months to 5 years (every 6 months). | Delivered through the government UIP/Anganwadi network. |
| **Weaning** | Introduce complementary foods around 6 months (not before 4). | Lead with iron-rich foods (ragi, lentils, pureed meats); practice responsive feeding. |
| **Honey** | **STRICTLY NO honey under 12 months.** | Infant botulism risk (a rare but fatal muscle paralysis). Do not use in *janam ghutti*. |
| **Juice** | **STRICTLY NO fruit juice under 12 months.** | Maximum of 4 oz/day of 100% whole fruit juice for children aged 1–3 years. |
| **Safe Sleep** | Back-to-sleep on a firm, flat, non-inclined surface. | No blankets, pillows, bumpers, soft toys, or weighted blankets. Room-share, don't bed-share. |
| **Swaddling** | Wrap loosely around hips; stop swaddling by 8 weeks. | Stop sooner if baby shows signs of rolling. Weighted swaddles are unsafe. |
| **Tummy Time** | Start supervised tummy time from day 1, building up. | Goal: 15–30 min/day by 7 weeks; 30–60 min/day by 3 months. |
| **Screen Time** | No screens under 18 months (except video chat). | 18–24 months: high-quality co-viewing; 2–5 years: limit to maximum 1 hour/day. |
| **Car Seats** | Rear-facing in the back seat until the seat limit is reached. | Keep children in the back seat until age 13. Walkers are not recommended. |
| **Bronchiolitis** | No routine salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, or chest X-rays. | Primary care is supportive: hydration, nasal saline, and suctioning. |
| **Croup** | Single-dose oral dexamethasone; **STRICTLY NO steam inhalation.** | Steam carries severe burn risk and has no proven clinical efficacy. |
| **Otitis Media** | Watchful waiting (48–72 hours) for children $\ge$ 2 years. | Antibiotics are required if under 2 years, bilateral, or severe. |
| **UTI** | Catheter or suprapubic sample required for culture. | Do not routinely perform VCUG/MCUG after a first simple febrile UTI. |
| **Diarrhoea** | Low-osmolarity ORS + Zinc (20 mg/day; 10 mg/day if <6 mo) for 14 days. | Continue breastfeeding and feeding. Avoid loperamide or anti-motility drugs. |
| **Antipyretics** | Single agent (paracetamol OR ibuprofen). | **Do NOT alternate drugs routinely. Do NOT cold-water sponge.** |
| **Aspirin** | **NEVER give aspirin to children under 19 years.** | Risk of Reye's Syndrome (life-threatening brain and liver swelling). |
| **CPR** | AHA 2025: C-A-B (Compressions-Airway-Breathing). | Compressions first. 30:2 ratio for a single rescuer. Depth: 1.5" (infant), 2" (child). |
| **Burns** | Cool under cool running tap water for at least 20 minutes. | **Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, oil, or henna.** |
| **Poisoning** | Call India NPIC (AIIMS New Delhi) at **1800-116-117** immediately. | **Do NOT induce vomiting.** Do not give fluids or charcoal unless instructed. |
| **Seizures** | Turn onto side (recovery position); clear area; protect head. | **Do NOT restrain baby. Do NOT insert anything in baby's mouth.** |
| **Preterm Care** | WHO 2022: Immediate skin-to-skin Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC). | Mother's own milk is default; pasteurised donor milk is the bridge. |
| **Autism** | Screen using M-CHAT-R/F at 18 and 24 months. | Diagnose using DSM-5-TR; adopt neurodiversity-affirming practices. |
| **HIV Exposure** | Maternal ART + undetectable viral load allows exclusive breastfeeding. | Breastfeed exclusively for 6 months; continue to 12 months+; no mixed feeding. |

---
content_html:
2. IAP & Pediatric Primary Care Standards

A summary of key clinical guidelines for non-vaccine medical and safety issues.

Topic IAP & Pediatric Standard Safety Callouts & Practical Advice
Breastfeeding Exclusive for 6 months; continue for 2 years or beyond. Aligns with WHO/UNICEF BFHI 2018. Initiate skin-to-skin during the "Golden Hour."
Vitamin D 400 IU/day oral Vitamin D3 starting from birth. Mandatory for all breastfed and partially breastfed infants.
Iron 1 mg/kg/day starting at 4 months for term breastfed babies. 2 mg/kg/day starting at 1 month for preterm/low-birth-weight babies.
Vitamin K 1 mg Vitamin K1 by intramuscular injection at birth. Universal birth dose to prevent Vitamin K Deficiency Bleeding (VKDB).
Vitamin A 9 doses from 9 months to 5 years (every 6 months). Delivered through the government UIP/Anganwadi network.
Weaning Introduce complementary foods around 6 months (not before 4). Lead with iron-rich foods (ragi, lentils, pureed meats); practice responsive feeding.
Honey STRICTLY NO honey under 12 months. Infant botulism risk (a rare but fatal muscle paralysis). Do not use in janam ghutti.
Juice STRICTLY NO fruit juice under 12 months. Maximum of 4 oz/day of 100% whole fruit juice for children aged 1–3 years.
Safe Sleep Back-to-sleep on a firm, flat, non-inclined surface. No blankets, pillows, bumpers, soft toys, or weighted blankets. Room-share, don't bed-share.
Swaddling Wrap loosely around hips; stop swaddling by 8 weeks. Stop sooner if baby shows signs of rolling. Weighted swaddles are unsafe.
Tummy Time Start supervised tummy time from day 1, building up. Goal: 15–30 min/day by 7 weeks; 30–60 min/day by 3 months.
Screen Time No screens under 18 months (except video chat). 18–24 months: high-quality co-viewing; 2–5 years: limit to maximum 1 hour/day.
Car Seats Rear-facing in the back seat until the seat limit is reached. Keep children in the back seat until age 13. Walkers are not recommended.
Bronchiolitis No routine salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, or chest X-rays. Primary care is supportive: hydration, nasal saline, and suctioning.
Croup Single-dose oral dexamethasone; STRICTLY NO steam inhalation. Steam carries severe burn risk and has no proven clinical efficacy.
Otitis Media Watchful waiting (48–72 hours) for children $\ge$ 2 years. Antibiotics are required if under 2 years, bilateral, or severe.
UTI Catheter or suprapubic sample required for culture. Do not routinely perform VCUG/MCUG after a first simple febrile UTI.
Diarrhoea Low-osmolarity ORS + Zinc (20 mg/day; 10 mg/day if <6 mo) for 14 days. Continue breastfeeding and feeding. Avoid loperamide or anti-motility drugs.
Antipyretics Single agent (paracetamol OR ibuprofen). Do NOT alternate drugs routinely. Do NOT cold-water sponge.
Aspirin NEVER give aspirin to children under 19 years. Risk of Reye's Syndrome (life-threatening brain and liver swelling).
CPR AHA 2025: C-A-B (Compressions-Airway-Breathing). Compressions first. 30:2 ratio for a single rescuer. Depth: 1.5" (infant), 2" (child).
Burns Cool under cool running tap water for at least 20 minutes. Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, oil, or henna.
Poisoning Call India NPIC (AIIMS New Delhi) at 1800-116-117 immediately. Do NOT induce vomiting. Do not give fluids or charcoal unless instructed.
Seizures Turn onto side (recovery position); clear area; protect head. Do NOT restrain baby. Do NOT insert anything in baby's mouth.
Preterm Care WHO 2022: Immediate skin-to-skin Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC). Mother's own milk is default; pasteurised donor milk is the bridge.
Autism Screen using M-CHAT-R/F at 18 and 24 months. Diagnose using DSM-5-TR; adopt neurodiversity-affirming practices.
HIV Exposure Maternal ART + undetectable viral load allows exclusive breastfeeding. Breastfeed exclusively for 6 months; continue to 12 months+; no mixed feeding.

chapter_title: Appendix A: IAP Standard Quick Reference Card
section_title: 3. IAP Guidelines on Congenital Heart Diseases (CHD) Timelines (IAP 2022)
content_md:
## 3. IAP Guidelines on Congenital Heart Diseases (CHD) Timelines (IAP 2022) *Timely diagnosis and intervention are critical for managing heart defects. Below is a parent-friendly summary of surgical and device closure timings according to the IAP 2022 Standard Treatment Guidelines.* | Heart Defect | Common Type / Classification | Ideal Age of Intervention / Closure | Standard Treatment Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ASD** | Atrial Septal Defect | • Asymptomatic: **2–4 years**
• Symptomatic in infancy: **Early** | • Device closure (if rims $\ge$ 5mm)
• Surgical repair (if rims < 5mm) | | **VSD** | Ventricular Septal Defect | • Small: **Observation only** (unless endocarditis/valve leak)
• Moderate: **2–5 years** (asymptomatic); **1–2 years** (medication-controlled)
• Large: **As early as possible / by 6 months** (prevents lung damage)
• Cusp Prolapse + AR: **Immediate surgery** | • Surgical patch repair (default)
• Device closure (for muscular/PM-VSD)
• PA banding (for complex/multiple VSDs) | | **PDA** | Patent Ductus Arteriosus | • Preterm: managed medically; closes spontaneously in 35–75%
• Small term: **12–18 months**
• Moderate/Large term: **By 3 months** | • Device closure (preferred if weight > 4–6 kg)
• Surgical ligation (if weight < 4 kg or unusual shape) | | **Complete AVSD** | Atrioventricular Septal Defect | • Controlled: **By 3 months**
• Uncontrolled heart failure: **As early as possible** | • Complete surgical repair | | **Partial AVSD** | Ostium Primum ASD + cleft MV | • **2–3 years** (or early if associated valve regurgitation) | • Surgical repair | | **APW** | Aortopulmonary Window | • Controlled: **By 3 months**
• Uncontrolled heart failure: **Immediate repair** | • Surgical patch repair | | **CoA** | Coarctation of the Aorta | • Weak heart/HTN/heart failure: **Immediate (neonatal)**
• Normal heart function + mild HTN: **3–6 months**
• Normal heart function + no HTN: **1–2 years** | • Surgical repair (infants)
• Balloon angioplasty (critically ill / <25 kg)
• Catheter stenting (>25 kg) | | **AS / PS** | Aortic / Pulmonary Stenosis | • Neonatal critical stenosis: **Immediate**
• Elective: **Peak gradient > 64 mmHg** or symptomatic | • Balloon dilation (preferred)
• Surgical valvotomy | | **TOF** | Tetralogy of Fallot | • Severe blue spells / cyanosis < 4 months: **Palliative BT shunt** or stent
• Symptomatic cyanosis: **4–6 months (Complete Repair)**
• Pink TOF: **6–12 months** | • Complete surgical repair (VSD closure + RVOT opening) | | **TGA** | Transposition of Great Arteries | • Simple TGA: **ASO (Arterial Switch) within 2–4 weeks** (or immediate)
• Complex TGA (with VSD/PDA): **6–8 weeks** | • Arterial Switch Operation (ASO) | | **TAPVC** | Total Anomalous Pulmonary Connection | • Obstructed TAPVC: **Emergency surgery at birth**
• Unobstructed: **Electively as early as possible** after diagnosis | • Surgical repair | | **Truncus Arteriosus** | Common Arterial Trunk | • Controlled: **3–6 weeks**
• Uncontrolled heart failure: **As early as possible** | • Surgical truncus repair (VSD closure + RV-PA conduit) | | **ALCAPA** | Anomalous Left Coronary Artery | • Presents at **2–4 months** with heart failure; **Immediate surgery** | • Surgical coronary translocation | ### Essential Cardiology Care & Follow-Up Rules: * **Infective Endocarditis (IE) Prevention:** Antibiotic prophylaxis (typically oral amoxicillin exactly 1 hour before dental/surgical procedures) is mandatory for **6 months** after any device or surgical closure of ASD, VSD, PDA, and AVSD. It is **life-long** for children with prosthetic valves, unrepaired cyanotic defects, or residual leaks. Maintain strict oral hygiene. * **Anticoagulation:** Children with prosthetic valves require oral anticoagulation (3 months for bioprosthetic, life-long for metallic). Track INR values as guided. * **Chest Care Post-Surgery:** Avoid pressure or lifting by the arms for 6–8 weeks after open-heart surgery. Avoid traditional chest massage on the healing sternum wound. ---
content_html:
3. IAP Guidelines on Congenital Heart Diseases (CHD) Timelines (IAP 2022)

Timely diagnosis and intervention are critical for managing heart defects. Below is a parent-friendly summary of surgical and device closure timings according to the IAP 2022 Standard Treatment Guidelines.

Heart Defect Common Type / Classification Ideal Age of Intervention / Closure Standard Treatment Method
ASD Atrial Septal Defect • Asymptomatic: 2–4 years
• Symptomatic in infancy: Early		 • Device closure (if rims $\ge$ 5mm)
• Surgical repair (if rims < 5mm)
VSD Ventricular Septal Defect • Small: Observation only (unless endocarditis/valve leak)
• Moderate: 2–5 years (asymptomatic); 1–2 years (medication-controlled)
• Large: As early as possible / by 6 months (prevents lung damage)
• Cusp Prolapse + AR: Immediate surgery		 • Surgical patch repair (default)
• Device closure (for muscular/PM-VSD)
• PA banding (for complex/multiple VSDs)
PDA Patent Ductus Arteriosus • Preterm: managed medically; closes spontaneously in 35–75%
• Small term: 12–18 months
• Moderate/Large term: By 3 months		 • Device closure (preferred if weight > 4–6 kg)
• Surgical ligation (if weight < 4 kg or unusual shape)
Complete AVSD Atrioventricular Septal Defect • Controlled: By 3 months
• Uncontrolled heart failure: As early as possible		 • Complete surgical repair
Partial AVSD Ostium Primum ASD + cleft MV • 2–3 years (or early if associated valve regurgitation) • Surgical repair
APW Aortopulmonary Window • Controlled: By 3 months
• Uncontrolled heart failure: Immediate repair		 • Surgical patch repair
CoA Coarctation of the Aorta • Weak heart/HTN/heart failure: Immediate (neonatal)
• Normal heart function + mild HTN: 3–6 months
• Normal heart function + no HTN: 1–2 years		 • Surgical repair (infants)
• Balloon angioplasty (critically ill / <25 kg)
• Catheter stenting (>25 kg)
AS / PS Aortic / Pulmonary Stenosis • Neonatal critical stenosis: Immediate
• Elective: Peak gradient > 64 mmHg or symptomatic		 • Balloon dilation (preferred)
• Surgical valvotomy
TOF Tetralogy of Fallot • Severe blue spells / cyanosis < 4 months: Palliative BT shunt or stent
• Symptomatic cyanosis: 4–6 months (Complete Repair)
• Pink TOF: 6–12 months		 • Complete surgical repair (VSD closure + RVOT opening)
TGA Transposition of Great Arteries • Simple TGA: ASO (Arterial Switch) within 2–4 weeks (or immediate)
• Complex TGA (with VSD/PDA): 6–8 weeks		 • Arterial Switch Operation (ASO)
TAPVC Total Anomalous Pulmonary Connection • Obstructed TAPVC: Emergency surgery at birth
• Unobstructed: Electively as early as possible after diagnosis		 • Surgical repair
Truncus Arteriosus Common Arterial Trunk • Controlled: 3–6 weeks
• Uncontrolled heart failure: As early as possible		 • Surgical truncus repair (VSD closure + RV-PA conduit)
ALCAPA Anomalous Left Coronary Artery • Presents at 2–4 months with heart failure; Immediate surgery • Surgical coronary translocation

Essential Cardiology Care & Follow-Up Rules:

  • Infective Endocarditis (IE) Prevention: Antibiotic prophylaxis (typically oral amoxicillin exactly 1 hour before dental/surgical procedures) is mandatory for 6 months after any device or surgical closure of ASD, VSD, PDA, and AVSD. It is life-long for children with prosthetic valves, unrepaired cyanotic defects, or residual leaks. Maintain strict oral hygiene.
  • Anticoagulation: Children with prosthetic valves require oral anticoagulation (3 months for bioprosthetic, life-long for metallic). Track INR values as guided.
  • Chest Care Post-Surgery: Avoid pressure or lifting by the arms for 6–8 weeks after open-heart surgery. Avoid traditional chest massage on the healing sternum wound.

chapter_title: Appendix A: IAP Standard Quick Reference Card
content_md:
## 4. Pediatric Emergency Red Flags

*Seek immediate medical care or go to the nearest emergency department if your baby shows any of the following:*

1. **Newborn Fever (under 3 months)**: Rectal temperature $\ge$ 100.4°F (38°C) is a medical emergency. Do not give paracetamol beforehand.
2. **Breathing Difficulty**: Breathing very fast, chest drawing in under the ribs, grunting, blue lips/face, or unable to feed.
3. **Dehydration**: No wet diaper for 6–8 hours, dry mouth, sunken eyes, sunken fontanelle (soft spot), or extreme lethargy.
4. **Seizures**: Any fit or convulsion, especially a first-ever seizure, one lasting longer than 5 minutes, or following a head injury.
5. **Choking**: Unable to cry, cough, or breathe; turning blue.
6. **Suspected Poisoning**: Swallowing household cleaners, medications, button batteries, or toxic plants. (Call NPIC at 1800-116-117).
7. **Burns**: Any burn on the face, hands, feet, or genitals, or any burn covering more than 5% of the body surface area.
8. **Head Injury**: Loss of consciousness, vomiting $\ge$ 2–3 times, fluid leaking from the nose/ears, unequal pupil sizes, or seizure.
9. **Poor Feeding**: Newborn refusing to feed, combined with severe lethargy or floppiness.
10. **Persistent Vomiting**: Vomiting repeatedly after every feed, or vomiting green/yellow bile.
11. **Anaphylaxis**: Severe allergic reaction with hives, facial swelling, wheezing, and difficulty breathing.

---

4. Pediatric Emergency Red Flags

  1. Newborn Fever (under 3 months): Rectal temperature $\ge$ 100.4°F (38°C) is a medical emergency. Do not give paracetamol beforehand.
  2. Breathing Difficulty: Breathing very fast, chest drawing in under the ribs, grunting, blue lips/face, or unable to feed.
  3. Dehydration: No wet diaper for 6–8 hours, dry mouth, sunken eyes, sunken fontanelle (soft spot), or extreme lethargy.
  4. Seizures: Any fit or convulsion, especially a first-ever seizure, one lasting longer than 5 minutes, or following a head injury.
  5. Choking: Unable to cry, cough, or breathe; turning blue.
  6. Suspected Poisoning: Swallowing household cleaners, medications, button batteries, or toxic plants. (Call NPIC at 1800-116-117).
  7. Burns: Any burn on the face, hands, feet, or genitals, or any burn covering more than 5% of the body surface area.
  8. Head Injury: Loss of consciousness, vomiting $\ge$ 2–3 times, fluid leaking from the nose/ears, unequal pupil sizes, or seizure.
  9. Poor Feeding: Newborn refusing to feed, combined with severe lethargy or floppiness.
  10. Persistent Vomiting: Vomiting repeatedly after every feed, or vomiting green/yellow bile.

## 5. Verified Emergency Helplines (India)

* **Single National Emergency Number**: **112**
* **Government Ambulance Service**: **108** or **102**
* **National Poisons Information Centre (AIIMS New Delhi)**: **1800-116-117** *(Toll-free, 24/7)*
* **Childline India**: **1098**
content_html:
5. Verified Emergency Helplines (India)

  • Single National Emergency Number: 112
  • Government Ambulance Service: 108 or 102
  • National Poisons Information Centre (AIIMS New Delhi): 1800-116-117 (Toll-free, 24/7)
  • Childline India: 1098
